title: 2006 Commonwealth Games

infobox.value: [Prince Edward, Earl of Wessex](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Duke_of_Edinburgh "Prince Edward, Duke of Edinburgh")

and commonly known as **Melbourne 2006**, were an [international](/wiki/International_sport "International sport") [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") for members of the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") held in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") between 15 and 26 March 2006. It was the fourth time Australia had hosted the Commonwealth Games. It was also the largest sporting event to be staged in Melbourne, eclipsing the [1956 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1956_Summer_Olympics "1956 Summer Olympics") in terms of the number of teams competing, [athletes](/wiki/Sportsperson "Sportsperson") competing, and events being held.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Multi-sport event in Melbourne, Australia

"Melbourne Commonwealth Games" redirects here. For the withdrawn bid for the games in Melbourne and across Victoria, see [Victoria bid for the 2026 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Victoria_bid_for_the_2026_Commonwealth_Games "Victoria bid for the 2026 Commonwealth Games").

The **2006 Commonwealth Games**, officially the **XVIII Commonwealth Games** and commonly known as **Melbourne 2006**, were an [international](/wiki/International_sport "International sport") [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") for members of the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") held in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") between 15 and 26 March 2006. It was the fourth time Australia had hosted the Commonwealth Games. It was also the largest sporting event to be staged in Melbourne, eclipsing the [1956 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1956_Summer_Olympics "1956 Summer Olympics") in terms of the number of teams competing, [athletes](/wiki/Sportsperson "Sportsperson") competing, and events being held.

More than 4,000 athletes from 71 Commonwealth Games Associations took part in the event.[[3]](#cite_note-3) [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe") withdrew its membership from the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") and [Commonwealth Games Federation](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Federation "Commonwealth Games Federation") on 8 December 2003 and so did not participate in the event.[[4]](#cite_note-4) With 245 sets of medals, the games featured 17 [Commonwealth sports](/wiki/Commonwealth_Sports "Commonwealth Sports"). These sporting events took place at 13 venues in the host city, two venues in [Bendigo](/wiki/Bendigo "Bendigo") and one venue each in [Ballarat](/wiki/Ballarat "Ballarat"), [Geelong](/wiki/Geelong "Geelong"), [Lysterfield Park](/wiki/Lysterfield_Park "Lysterfield Park") and [Traralgon](/wiki/Traralgon "Traralgon").[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)

The site for the opening and closing ceremonies was the [Melbourne Cricket Ground](/wiki/Melbourne_Cricket_Ground "Melbourne Cricket Ground"), which was also used during the 1956 Olympics.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The [mascot](/wiki/Mascot "Mascot") for the games was [Karak](/wiki/Karak_(mascot) "Karak (mascot)"), a [red-tailed black cockatoo](/wiki/Red-tailed_black_cockatoo "Red-tailed black cockatoo") (a [threatened species](/wiki/Threatened_species "Threatened species")).[[7]](#cite_note-7) The official song of the games, "[Together We Are One](/wiki/Together_We_Are_One "Together We Are One")", was composed by the [ARIA](/wiki/ARIA_Music_Awards "ARIA Music Awards") awardee Australian recording artist [Delta Goodrem](/wiki/Delta_Goodrem "Delta Goodrem"). During the closing ceremony of the games, President of the Commonwealth Games Federation Mike Fennell declared to the crowd "Melbourne, you are simply the best".[[8]](#cite_note-8)

For the first time in the history of the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"), the Queen's Baton visited every single Commonwealth nation and territory taking part in the Games, a journey of 180,000 kilometres (110,000 miles). The relay ended when the Governor of Victoria, and former Commonwealth Games medallist, John Landy delivered the baton to Her Majesty the Queen at the Melbourne Cricket Ground during the opening ceremony.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The host nation Australia topped the medal table for the fifth time in the past five Commonwealth Games, winning the most golds (84) and most medals overall (221). [England](/wiki/England_at_the_2018_Commonwealth_Games "England at the 2018 Commonwealth Games") and Canada finished second and third respectively.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The 2006 Commonwealth Games have been lauded as “best Commonwealth Games ever”.[[11]](#cite_note-11) A KPMG analysis of 2006 Commonwealth Games found the event prompted an increase in gross state product of about [A$](/wiki/Australian_dollar "Australian dollar")1.6 billion over a 20-year period and employment of about 13,600 jobs.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Host selection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Host selection")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Melbourne_CBD_2008.jpg/250px-Melbourne_CBD_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Melbourne_CBD_2008.jpg)

Melbourne was selected by the [Australian Commonwealth Games Association](/wiki/Australian_Commonwealth_Games_Association "Australian Commonwealth Games Association") as the official bid city from Australia for the 2006 Commonwealth Games

Having previously hosted the 1956 Olympics, [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne") has hosted world sporting events such as the [1979 World Fencing Championships](/wiki/1979_World_Fencing_Championships "1979 World Fencing Championships"). The city made a bid to host the [1996 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1996_Summer_Olympics "1996 Summer Olympics"), but was unsuccessful in its bid to [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta").[[13]](#cite_note-13) Following the failure of the Olympic bid, the city would later make plans to host the Commonwealth Games. During the [1998 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1998_Commonwealth_Games "1998 Commonwealth Games") in [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), two cities initially expressed interest in hosting the event; Melbourne, Australia and [Wellington](/wiki/Wellington "Wellington"), New Zealand.[[14]](#cite_note-14) [Wellington](/wiki/Wellington "Wellington") withdrew its bid, citing the costs involved with matching the bid plan presented by Melbourne, which became the default host without members of the Federation going to vote.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Cost

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Cost")]

Early concerns arose about the large cost of staging the event, with projected costs likely to be over AUD 1 billion and a high likelihood the Victorian taxpayer would have to cover the expense. The cost was described in some local media as excessive. [National Party](/wiki/National_Party_of_Australia "National Party of Australia") leader [Peter Ryan](/wiki/Peter_Ryan_(politician) "Peter Ryan (politician)") said that the Labor government should win "gold (medal) for burning money".[[18]](#cite_note-18) However, not all of this money was wasted. The actual costs for hosting the games was AUD 1.144 billion and prior to the Games, accountants at [KPMG](/wiki/KPMG "KPMG") were estimating that the gross income generated by this event could be as high as AUD 1.5 billion.

### Athletes' village

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Athletes' village")]

An athlete's village in the inner suburb of [Parkville](/wiki/Parkville,_Victoria "Parkville, Victoria") housed approximately 7,000 athletes and support staff during the Games, and has been transformed into commercial housing with a distinctly eco-friendly image. The creation of this village attracted controversy, with critics claiming it was created by alienating public parkland, while proponents maintained that it represented the renewal of an otherwise derelict inner-city area.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Development")]

The change from [Daylight Saving Time](/wiki/Daylight_Saving_Time "Daylight Saving Time") to Standard Time in Australian states that follow it was delayed from 26 March to 2 April for 2006 to avoid affecting the games. In addition, state and private schools amended their usual term times so as to allow the first term holidays to coincide with the Games.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Melbourne's public transport system – train, tram and bus – ran to altered timetables with some amended or substituted services for the duration of the Games. For the most part, timetabled services were unchanged but suffered due to higher loads.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

For the first time ever, the Melbourne 2006 Commonwealth Games appointed a Goodwill Partner, [Plan International Australia](/wiki/Plan_(aid_organisation) "Plan (aid organisation)").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Opening ceremony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Opening ceremony")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Yarra_River_Fireworks.jpeg/250px-Yarra_River_Fireworks.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Yarra_River_Fireworks.jpeg)

Fireworks at the [Yarra River](/wiki/Yarra_River "Yarra River") during the opening ceremony of the 2006 Commonwealth Games

Main article: [2006 Commonwealth Games opening ceremony](/wiki/2006_Commonwealth_Games_opening_ceremony "2006 Commonwealth Games opening ceremony")

Both the [Melbourne Cricket Ground](/wiki/Melbourne_Cricket_Ground "Melbourne Cricket Ground") and the [Yarra River](/wiki/Yarra_River "Yarra River") were centrepieces for the ceremony, which included many fireworks, and other spectacle. The Games were opened by [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II"), in her capacity as [Head of the Commonwealth](/wiki/Head_of_the_Commonwealth "Head of the Commonwealth"). The Queen is also [Head of State](/wiki/Head_of_State "Head of State") of a number of Commonwealth countries.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Both the [Melbourne Cricket Ground](/wiki/Melbourne_Cricket_Ground "Melbourne Cricket Ground") and the [Yarra River](/wiki/Yarra_River "Yarra River") were again centrepieces for the ceremony. [Samresh Jung](/wiki/Samresh_Jung "Samresh Jung") of India was given the [David Dixon Award](/wiki/David_Dixon_Award "David Dixon Award") at the closing ceremony. ## Broadcasting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Broadcasting")]

* The host broadcaster was [Trans World International](/wiki/Trans_World_International "Trans World International"), while the domestic rights-holding broadcaster was the [Nine Network](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network") in Australia. They showed rolling coverage, except for a break for the [evening news](/wiki/Nine_News "Nine News") and overnight.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)
* In Australia [Fox Sports](/wiki/Fox_Sports_(Australia) "Fox Sports (Australia)") broadcast the Games on eight dedicated digital [Pay-TV](/wiki/Pay-TV "Pay-TV") channels. These were available on the [Foxtel](/wiki/Foxtel "Foxtel"), [Austar](/wiki/Austar "Austar") and [Optus Vision](/wiki/Optus_Vision "Optus Vision") networks.
* The [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") covered the Commonwealth Games in the United Kingdom on [BBC One](/wiki/BBC_One "BBC One") and [BBC Two](/wiki/BBC_Two "BBC Two"). [BBCi](/wiki/BBC_Red_Button "BBC Red Button") included a choice of two extra video streams on [Freeview](/wiki/Freeview_(UK) "Freeview (UK)") and four streams on Digital [Satellite](/wiki/Satellite "Satellite") and [Cable](/wiki/Cable_television "Cable television").[[27]](#cite_note-27) Users with broadband in the United Kingdom could also view all 5 video streams on [bbc.co.uk](/wiki/Bbc.co.uk "Bbc.co.uk"), and the BBC Sport website.
* [CBC](/wiki/Canadian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Canadian Broadcasting Corporation"), [CBC Newsworld](/wiki/CBC_Newsworld "CBC Newsworld"), and [CBC Country Canada](/wiki/CBC_Country_Canada "CBC Country Canada") aired a daily one-hour highlights show of the Commonwealth Games in Canada.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Compared to past games, the CBC's coverage was minimally staffed, with commentary from other broadcasting partners. At first, they did not even consider bidding for the broadcasting rights[[29]](#cite_note-29) due to scheduling conflicts with events Canadians are more interested in, such as the [Tim Hortons Brier](/wiki/Tim_Hortons_Brier "Tim Hortons Brier"), [World Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/World_Figure_Skating_Championships "World Figure Skating Championships"), and the [2006 Winter Paralympics](/wiki/2006_Winter_Paralympics "2006 Winter Paralympics") *(which itself had been reduced to five-to-ten-minute daily coverage)*. None of Canada's metropolitan newspapers sent any journalists to report on the Games, instead relying on news agencies
* [TVNZ](/wiki/TVNZ "TVNZ") covered the games for the residents of New Zealand.[[30]](#cite_note-30)
* In [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [TV1](/wiki/TV1_(Malaysia) "TV1 (Malaysia)") broadcast live coverage of the Games for three hours starting at 10 am Malaysian time and for two hours starting at 3 pm, with highlights at 12:30 am. Satellite provider [Astro](/wiki/Astro_(satellite_TV) "Astro (satellite TV)") included three dedicated channels to broadcast the Games live to its Sports package subscribers, in addition to delayed broadcast 24 hours later.
* Singapore's [MediaCorp](/wiki/MediaCorp "MediaCorp") TV had supposedly not broadcast the games due to the high cost of [telecast](/wiki/Broadcasting "Broadcasting") rights, [satellite](/wiki/Satellite "Satellite") charges and the lack of [sponsors](/wiki/Sponsor_(commercial) "Sponsor (commercial)"). However, on 17 March, the MediaCorp found other sponsors which is the [Ministry of Community Development, Youth and Sports](/wiki/Ministry_of_Community_Development,_Youth_and_Sports "Ministry of Community Development, Youth and Sports") and the [Singapore Sports Council](/wiki/Singapore_Sports_Council "Singapore Sports Council"). Broadcast started from 18 March until the end of the games.
* In India, [Prasar Bharati](/wiki/Prasar_Bharati "Prasar Bharati") broadcast the games on [DD National](/wiki/DD_National "DD National") and [DD Sports](/wiki/DD_Sports "DD Sports"). All India Radio broadcast the running commentary of the main matches and events.[[31]](#cite_note-31)
* In the United States, selected coverage was carried by [Fox College Sports](/wiki/Fox_College_Sports "Fox College Sports").
* In [the Bahamas](/wiki/The_Bahamas "The Bahamas"), Cable 12 on cable Bahamas broadcast the games.[[32]](#cite_note-32)
* In Europe, coverage was carried by [EBU](/wiki/European_Broadcasting_Union "European Broadcasting Union") members.
* Altogether an estimated 4 billion viewers watched the 2006 Commonwealth Games worldwide.

## Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Controversies")]

### Missing athletes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Missing athletes")]

On 20 March 2006 it was reported that two athletes had gone missing from the Commonwealth Games village: [Tanzanian](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania") boxer [Omari Idd Kimweri](/wiki/Omari_Idd_Kimweri "Omari Idd Kimweri") and [Bangladeshi](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") runner Mohammad Tawhidul Islam.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

On 22 March 2006 it was reported that seven athletes from [Sierra Leone](/wiki/Sierra_Leone "Sierra Leone") (three women and four men) had also disappeared. A further seven Sierra Leonean athletes also went missing during the course of the Games, bringing the total runaway count to fourteen (two-thirds of the team). [Victoria Police](/wiki/Victoria_Police "Victoria Police") believed that they had fled to Sydney where the Sierra Leonean community is much larger than Melbourne's.

Two hours before the Closing Ceremony on 26 March, officials from the [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon "Cameroon") team reported to police that nine of their members had also vanished.

These incidents were not without precedent: 27 athletes similarly disappeared from the [2002 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2002_Commonwealth_Games "2002 Commonwealth Games") in Manchester, England (21 from Sierra Leone, 5 from Bangladesh and one from Pakistan), and over 80 athletes and officials overstayed their visas after the [2000 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2000_Summer_Olympics "2000 Summer Olympics") in Sydney.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

On request of Sierra Leone officials, the Commonwealth Games Federation cancelled those athletes' Games accreditation, allowing the [Australian Department of Immigration and Multicultural Affairs](/wiki/Department_of_Immigration_and_Multicultural_Affairs_(Australia) "Department of Immigration and Multicultural Affairs (Australia)") (DIMA) to cancel their visas at midnight on 27 March, and begin investigating their disappearance.
At 7.20 am on that day, [New South Wales Police](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Police "New South Wales Police") located six of the Sierra Leonean athletes in a house at [Freshwater](/wiki/Freshwater,_New_South_Wales "Freshwater, New South Wales") near [Manly Beach](/wiki/Manly_Beach "Manly Beach") in Sydney. All six indicated they wished to seek [political asylum in Australia](/wiki/Political_asylum_in_Australia "Political asylum in Australia"), and were granted bridging visas by DIMA while their refugee applications were arranged. The athletes claimed to have been subjected to violence and torture in their home country; seventeen-year-old Isha Conteh stated she could be forced into [female genital cutting](/wiki/Female_genital_cutting "Female genital cutting") if she returned.[[40]](#cite_note-40) On Tuesday 28 March, six further Sierra Leoneans turned themselves in to immigration authorities in Sydney and were also granted bridging visas.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Two of the missing Cameroonian athletes were later found in [Perth, Western Australia](/wiki/Perth,_Western_Australia "Perth, Western Australia").

### The Stolenwealth Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2006_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: The Stolenwealth Games")]

The Stolenwealth Games were protests at the 1982 and 2006 Commonwealth Games. People were protesting because they believed that the Commonwealth Games led to the erasure of indigenous people.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43) There were also problems with land rights. These protests led to the creation of the Global Indigenous Games.

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** The four [Home Nations](/wiki/Home_Nations "Home Nations") of the United Kingdom — England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland — send separate teams to the Commonwealth Games, as do the three [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_dependency "Crown dependency") — [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey "Jersey"), the [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man") and [Guernsey](/wiki/Guernsey "Guernsey") — and 9 of the 14 [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories"). The [Cook Islands](/wiki/Cook_Islands "Cook Islands") and [Niue](/wiki/Niue "Niue"), non-sovereign territories in [free association](/wiki/Associated_state "Associated state") with New Zealand also compete separately. There are thus 53 members of the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"), but 71 competing teams at the Commonwealth Games.
